preFix.title: Polymer clay miniature cacti trio
preFix.subtitle: Three sculpted cactus figures in a shared pot
preFix.excerpt: Three miniature cactus forms in different shapes built from green polymer clay and planted in a shared air-dry clay pot. The cacti are hand-sculpted, textured with a needle tool to suggest spines, and planted before the pot clay dries. The finished piece is approximately 60 mm tall and is purely decorative.
preFix.sourceNotes: Miniature sculpture technique derived from first principles.

content.text: Three miniature cactus figures combined in one small pot make a desk or shelf piece that needs no watering. The cacti are sculpted from
polymer clay

and planted into an air-dry clay pot before the pot clay dries. The pot clay grips the cactus stems as it hardens, fixing them in place. No kiln required for the cacti; the pot hardens at room temperature.
Building the pot

Pinch a small air-dry clay pot approximately 40 mm diameter and 30 mm tall. The pot should be open at the top with walls about 5 mm thick. Set it aside; it will accept the cactus stems while still soft.
Sculpting the three cacti
Cactus 1: a columnar form. Roll mid-green clay into a cylinder 10 mm in diameter and 50 mm tall. Insert a short length of wire through the centre as an
armature
for the wire to extend 10 mm from the base as a planting stem. Use the needle tool to press shallow vertical ridges down the length of the cylinder at 3 mm intervals.

Cactus 2: a paddle cactus. Roll two flat oval discs of dark green, approximately 20 mm long and 12 mm wide, each 5 mm thick. Press them together at a slight angle to make a two-paddle form. Flatten a small base and press the paddles upright onto it. Insert a wire stem into the base.

Cactus 3: a round barrel cactus. Roll a ball of mid-green clay 20 mm in diameter. Flatten the base slightly. Use the needle tool to press ribs around the circumference at 4 mm intervals. Add a small flat circle of lighter green on top as a crown detail. Insert a wire stem into the base.
Adding spine texture

Press small dots with the needle tool along the ridges of the columnar and barrel cacti. Each dot represents a spine cluster. On the paddle cactus, press clusters of three dots at regular intervals across the paddle surface. The dots do not need to be deep; a light prick makes a convincing spine impression.
Curing the cacti
Bake the three cactus forms on a dedicated oven liner at 110-130°C for 20-25 minutes. The wire armatures do not need to be removed before baking. Allow the cured cacti to cool fully.
Planting

While the air-dry clay pot is still soft, push the wire stems of each cactus into the pot clay. Arrange the three cacti at slightly different angles. The wire stems anchor in the soft clay and remain fixed as the pot hardens over 24-48 hours.
Finishing

Once the pot is bone-dry, add a surface of fine sand or fine gravel pressed into the exposed clay surface around the cactus bases to suggest soil. Seal the pot with clear acrylic varnish. Apply a wash of watered-down yellow-green acrylic paint over the cactus surfaces for subtle colour variation.
Variations
Add tiny polymer clay flowers to the tops of the cacti before curing: pink or yellow discs pressed onto the crown.
Build a larger pot and add five or six cacti in different shapes for a desert scene display.
Make succulent-style pieces with flat, layered petal forms radiating from a central point as an alternative to cacti.

rows.fix: Allow the pot to stiffen slightly before planting taller forms. Use a longer wire stem for more anchorage.
rows.cause: Wire armature too short or clay pot too soft when planted
rows.problem: Taller cactus leans to one side after planting

rows.fix: Insert a wire armature through the full length of tall cactus forms before curing.
rows.cause: Wire armature absent or insufficient, making the cactus fragile at the join
rows.problem: Cactus breaks at the base when handled

rows.fix: Dry the pot slowly under a loose plastic cover for the first 12 hours. Small cracks can be filled with fresh air-dry clay before the pot is fully dry.
rows.cause: Clay dried too fast and shrank around the rigid wire
rows.problem: Pot cracks as it dries around the wire stems

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
brand-trademark: brand name "Anchor" — generic equivalent is "salted butter"
paragraph reads at grade 11.1 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
